questions.question: Where should we store transcript files on KVM - in the database as text content (like artifacts) or as files on disk with static file serving?

options.label: Database (inline content)
options.description: Store transcript content as TEXT in database. Simple, no file I/O, automatic versioning. Good for transcripts under ~1MB.

options.label: Filesystem (disk storage)
options.description: Store as files in /opt/stack/AIStudio/storage/transcripts/. Better for large files, requires Express static serving setup.

questions.question: When should transcripts be copied from laptop to KVM?

options.label: Immediately on completion
options.description: Copy transcript when agent completes (record_agent_complete). Proactive, transcripts always available, but uses more storage.

options.label: On-demand viewing
options.description: Copy only when user clicks 'View Transcript'. Saves storage, but requires user to wait for transfer.

questions.question: How should transcripts be displayed in the UI?

options.label: Raw JSONL format
options.description: Show the raw transcript file as-is. Simple, preserves all data, but less user-friendly.

options.label: Parsed and formatted
options.description: Parse JSONL and display conversation turns, tool calls, and responses in a readable format. Better UX, requires parsing logic.

questions.question: What size transcripts do you expect? This affects storage and transfer approach.

options.label: Small (<100KB)
options.description: Typical short sessions. Database storage is fine.

options.label: Medium (100KB-1MB)
options.description: Most sessions. Either approach works.

options.label: Large (1MB-10MB+)
options.description: Long sessions or many tool calls. Filesystem storage recommended.

questions.question: Should the daemon run as a separate process or integrate with the existing remote agent (laptop-agent)?

options.label: Separate daemon process
options.description: New standalone service that watches transcripts. Simpler, dedicated purpose, but adds another process to manage.

options.label: Integrate with remote agent
options.description: Add 'watch-transcripts' capability to existing laptop-agent. Reuses infrastructure, single process, but more complex.

questions.question: How should the daemon match new transcripts to workflow runs?

options.label: Parse transcript content
options.description: Read first few lines of new transcript to extract sessionId/agentId, then match to active workflows. More reliable but requires file I/O.

options.label: Watch running-workflows.json
options.description: Monitor .claude/running-workflows.json for session registrations, match by filename. Faster but relies on hook tracking.

options.label: Hybrid approach
options.description: Watch running-workflows.json first, fall back to parsing transcript content if no match found. Best of both worlds.

questions.question: When should the daemon call add_transcript?

options.label: Immediately on file creation
options.description: Register as soon as new .jsonl file appears. Fastest, but might register non-agent files.

options.label: After first write (debounced)
options.description: Wait for file to have content (200ms debounce). Ensures file is valid before registering.

options.label: After validating content
options.description: Read first line to confirm it's a valid transcript. Most reliable but slowest.

questions.question: How should next_step behave: always return current step (requiring explicit 'advance' call), or have a parameter to mark previous complete and advance?

options.label: Auto-advance
options.description: next_step always advances to next step (assumes previous is done)

options.label: Explicit parameter
options.description: markCurrentComplete parameter controls whether to advance

options.label: Separate tools
options.description: get_current_step (read-only) + advance_step (moves forward)

questions.question: Should these tools work independently from the existing Docker-based Story Runner, or integrate with it?

options.label: Independent
options.description: Any LLM can orchestrate directly via MCP, no Docker runner needed

options.label: Integrated
options.description: Works alongside Docker runner, shares state via checkpoints

options.label: Replace runner
options.description: New tools replace the Docker runner entirely

questions.question: How should approval gates be handled when encountered?

options.label: Return prompt
options.description: Return instructions telling orchestrator to call respond_to_approval

options.label: Block until resolved
options.description: Tool waits/polls until approval is given, then returns next step

options.label: Skip/flag
options.description: Flag in response that approval is needed, orchestrator decides

questions.question: Which parts of this plan should we prioritize for the first implementation?

options.label: All together
options.description: Implement all 4 solutions as one cohesive change

options.label: Step commands first
options.description: Focus on get_current_step/advance_step/repeat_step only

options.label: Story key first
options.description: Add story key resolution to existing tools first (quick win)

options.label: Session context first
options.description: Add set_context to eliminate repeated projectId

questions.question: What type of implementation do you want for ST-190?

options.label: Execute Live Test
options.description: Run a workflow execution now to manually verify transcript registration works (I'll execute the story with a team and verify transcripts)

options.label: Write Automated E2E Tests
options.description: Create Playwright E2E tests that can be run repeatedly to verify transcript registration

options.label: Both
options.description: Execute a live test now AND create automated E2E tests for future regression testing

questions.question: Which team should be used for testing?

options.label: Simplified Dev Workflow
options.description: 3 states (exploration → implementation → testing) - faster execution, good for quick verification

options.label: Standard Dev Workflow
options.description: 9 states - more comprehensive but longer execution time

options.label: Create Minimal Test Workflow
options.description: Create a new minimal 1-state workflow specifically designed for transcript testing

questions.question: Where should these workflow control buttons appear?

options.label: WorkflowExecutionMonitor only
options.description: Add buttons to the existing workflow monitor page (/workflow-runs/:runId)

options.label: StoryDetailPage only
options.description: Add buttons to the story detail page, visible when viewing a story

options.label: Both pages
options.description: Add buttons to both pages for maximum accessibility

questions.question: For 'Mark phase to skip' - what should this do?

options.label: Skip future phases
options.description: Mark a not-yet-executed state to be skipped when the runner reaches it

options.label: Skip current and continue
options.description: Skip the currently running/paused phase and move to the next state

options.label: Both options
options.description: Provide UI to skip current phase OR mark future phases to skip

questions.question: Should the 'Run workflow' button replace or complement the existing story execution path?

options.label: New control panel
options.description: Add a dedicated 'Workflow Controls' panel with all 4 buttons grouped together

options.label: Header actions
options.description: Add buttons to the page header alongside existing Refresh/Back buttons

options.label: Inline with states
options.description: Add control buttons contextually next to workflow states

questions.question: After implementing Skott integration, how should we handle existing files in the database that have no dependency data?

options.label: Run full project analysis
options.description: Queue a full analyze-project job to populate all files with dependency data (~few minutes)

options.label: Lazy populate on access
options.description: Only populate when get_file_dependencies is called for a specific file

options.label: No backfill needed
options.description: Just let new commits/analyses populate data going forward

questions.question: Which workflow should I use for ST-196?

options.label: Standard with Approved Plan
options.description: 6 states - Security → Architecture → Approval → TDD → Implementation → Review (recommended since plan exists)

options.label: Simplified Dev Workflow
options.description: 3 states - Quick exploration → implementation → testing

options.label: Skip workflow, implement directly
options.description: No workflow tracking - just implement the plan now

questions.question: Do you approve proceeding with the implementation of ST-196 (Skott integration)?

options.label: Approve
options.description: Proceed with implementation as planned

options.label: Approve with changes
options.description: Proceed but I have specific modifications

options.label: Reject
options.description: Do not proceed - need more analysis

questions.question: How should the Master CLI session receive MCP commands? The story suggests injecting text prompts like 'Call record_agent_start with...' but there's an alternative approach using Claude Code's native MCP bridge. Which approach do you prefer?

options.label: Text Prompt Injection
options.description: Runner sends natural language prompts to Claude CLI asking it to call MCP tools. Claude parses and executes. More flexible but requires result parsing.

options.label: Direct MCP via CLI stdin
options.description: Use Claude Code's --mcp-server flag to register VibeStudio MCP, then send tool calls directly. More reliable but requires CLI MCP support verification.

questions.question: Should we keep the AgentSession class for backward compatibility or fully remove it in favor of Task tool spawning?

options.label: Remove AgentSession
options.description: Fully commit to Task tool approach. Simpler architecture but no fallback.

options.label: Keep as Fallback
options.description: Keep AgentSession for edge cases where Task tool doesn't work (e.g., specific tool restrictions).

questions.question: Based on my explanation, which MCP execution approach should we use for ST-200?

options.label: Text Prompt Injection
options.description: Send natural language prompts asking Claude to call MCP tools. Aligned with story description and existing pre/post instruction flow.

options.label: Direct MCP Protocol
options.description: Use Claude Code's native MCP protocol for structured tool calls. More deterministic but requires verifying CLI support.

questions.question: Should we update ST-200 to use your existing laptop agent infrastructure (spawn_agent MCP) instead of Task tool spawning?

options.label: Use Laptop Agent
options.description: Master session calls spawn_agent MCP → work dispatched to your laptop via WebSocket. Uses existing infrastructure.

options.label: Keep Task Tool
options.description: Keep original plan with Task tool. Would require installing Claude Code in Docker container on KVM.
